title.en: Choosing Your AI Platform: Foundry vs Bedrock vs Vertex

description.en: Compare Azure AI Foundry, Amazon Bedrock, and Google Vertex AI as enterprise AI platforms, and build a weighted-criteria scorer that turns platform selection into an auditable decision.

story_variants.en:
## The Decision Everyone Has an Opinion About

No architecture decision in 2025-2026 attracts more lobbying than the enterprise AI platform. The data team wants Vertex because the warehouse is BigQuery. The apps team wants Foundry because the company lives in Microsoft 365 and Entra. The platform team wants Bedrock because everything else runs on AWS. Everyone is *locally* right — which is precisely why the decision needs a framework instead of a shouting match.

First, sharpen what is being chosen. All three are **managed AI platforms**: model catalogs behind one API, fine-tuning and evaluation tooling, agent frameworks, safety filters, and enterprise controls (private networking, CMK encryption, audit logging, IAM integration). You are not choosing a model — models are increasingly available in several places at once. You are choosing the **operational home** for AI workloads: where quotas, governance, observability, and invoices live.

## The Three Contenders

- **Azure AI Foundry** (the evolution of Azure OpenAI Service into a unified platform): the strongest pull for OpenAI-model access under enterprise terms, deep Entra ID integration, Microsoft 365/Copilot ecosystem adjacency, agent tooling, and a growing multi-vendor catalog (including Anthropic, Meta, Mistral, xAI and others alongside OpenAI).
- **Amazon Bedrock**: the multi-vendor pioneer — Anthropic's Claude, Meta's Llama, Mistral, Cohere, Amazon's own Nova family and more behind one API; Bedrock Agents and AgentCore for agentic workloads; Guardrails for safety; Knowledge Bases for managed RAG; tight IAM/VPC/PrivateLink integration for AWS-native estates.
- **Vertex AI**: Google's full ML platform with Gemini at the center plus a broad Model Garden (including Claude and open models); strongest when the story is *data + AI* — BigQuery adjacency, embeddings and Vector Search, mature MLOps (pipelines, feature store, model registry), and TPU economics for training-heavy shops.

| Dimension | Azure AI Foundry | Amazon Bedrock | Vertex AI |
|---|---|---|---|
| Signature model pull | OpenAI (GPT family) + multi-vendor catalog | Claude + broad multi-vendor choice | Gemini + Model Garden |
| Identity & access | Entra ID native | AWS IAM native | Cloud IAM native |
| Managed RAG offering | Foundry + Azure AI Search | Knowledge Bases | Vertex AI Search / RAG Engine |
| Agent tooling | Foundry Agent Service | Bedrock Agents / AgentCore | Vertex AI Agent Builder / ADK |
| Data platform adjacency | Fabric / Synapse | Redshift / S3 lakes | BigQuery (strongest) |
| Where it wins by default | Microsoft-centric enterprises | AWS-native estates wanting model choice | Data/ML-first organizations |

> **Callout:** The quiet truth: for most enterprises the AI platform decision is 70% decided by *where you already are* — identity, data gravity, and platform skills — and 30% by AI-specific differentiators. Pretending otherwise produces decks, not deployments.

## The Criteria That Actually Discriminate

Score candidates on weighted criteria; weight by *your* context, not vendor marketing:

- **Model access under your terms** — the specific models you need, with the data-use commitments (no training on your prompts), regional availability, and quota you need.
- **Enterprise integration** — how much identity, networking, and governance work is already done because the platform matches your anchor cloud.
- **Data residency & compliance** — regional processing guarantees for prompts and stored artifacts; alignment with obligations like the EU AI Act's rollout (its GPAI obligations began applying in 2025) and your GDPR posture from earlier lessons.
- **Cost flexibility** — provisioned vs on-demand throughput, batch pricing, caching discounts, and — critically — the *egress cost of feeding it your data* if the platform sits off your anchor cloud.
- **Ecosystem trajectory** — agent frameworks, evaluation tooling, and interoperability standards (MCP for tool integrations, agent-to-agent protocols) that hedge against tomorrow's requirements.

## Failure Modes of Platform Selection

- **The benchmark trap.** Choosing on a leaderboard delta that next quarter's model releases erase. Platform choice outlives any model ranking; optimize for the platform, keep the model swappable behind an abstraction (the gateway pattern — final lesson).
- **The two-platform accident.** One team ships on Bedrock, another on Foundry, before governance notices; now safety filters, logging, and cost controls exist twice, inconsistently. Multi-platform is a legitimate *strategy* (data gravity may genuinely split you); it should not be an *accident*.
- **Ignoring the throughput fine print.** POC succeeds at 10 requests/minute; production needs provisioned throughput with capacity planning, quotas, and regional availability nobody checked. Capacity is an architectural input, not a launch-week surprise.
- **Sub-processor amnesia.** Legal signed data-processing terms for the anchor cloud; the AI platform's model providers were never added to the sub-processor list. Your compliance lesson's questionnaire returns with sharper teeth.

## Making It Auditable

The deliverable is a **scoring matrix**: criteria, weights agreed *before* scoring (to prevent motivated arithmetic), per-platform scores with written justification, and a recorded decision with revisit triggers ("re-evaluate if we migrate the warehouse", "if model X becomes unavailable"). This is exactly what you will build in the challenge: a weighted scorer over Foundry, Bedrock, and Vertex for a Microsoft-centric enterprise — where, unsurprisingly, integration weight moves the answer. Change the weights and watch the winner change: that sensitivity, made visible, is the entire point of doing it with code instead of vibes.

description.en: Where you already are — identity, data, skills — dominates the platform decision; the gateway abstraction keeps the model layer swappable afterwards.

left.label: Pick the platform from this month's model leaderboard

right.label: Weighted criteria agreed before scoring: integration, residency, cost, model terms

description.en: Model rankings churn quarterly; platform choice lasts years. Weight your context, score in the open, record the decision.

explanation.en: Weights encode YOUR context — a Microsoft-centric enterprise weights integration differently than a data-first one.
Each platform gets per-criterion scores with written justification — the arithmetic is the easy part.
Sum of weight times score: transparent, auditable, and trivially re-runnable when weights or scores change.

description.en: Auditable platform selection: weights before scores, decision with triggers, abstraction after.

lines: sensitivity: +1 integration weight -> foundry lead widens

description.en: A close race decided by integration weight — exactly the sensitivity a review board should see.

instruction.en: Build the weighted AI-platform scorer for a Microsoft-centric enterprise. Implement weighted_score(platform_scores) -> int: the sum over WEIGHTS of weight * platform_scores[criterion]. Compute each platform's total, print one line per platform as '<platform>: <score>' sorted by score DESCENDING (scores are distinct), then print 'Recommended platform: <winner>'. Weighted scoring is what turns the most lobbied decision in enterprise AI into an auditable artifact.

practice_challenges.expected_output:
foundry: 33
bedrock: 32
vertex: 30
Recommended platform: foundry

en: weighted_score: sum(WEIGHTS[c] * platform_scores[c] for c in WEIGHTS).
Build totals = {name: weighted_score(s) for name, s in PLATFORMS.items()} then sort its items by value descending.
sorted(totals.items(), key=lambda kv: -kv[1]) gives the ranking; the winner is the first element.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert weighted_score({"model_choice": 4, "enterprise_integration": 5, "data_residency": 4, "cost_flexibility": 3}) == 33
assert weighted_score({"model_choice": 4, "enterprise_integration": 4, "data_residency": 4, "cost_flexibility": 4}) == 32
assert weighted_score({"model_choice": 4, "enterprise_integration": 3, "data_residency": 4, "cost_flexibility": 4}) == 30
assert weighted_score({"model_choice": 0, "enterprise_integration": 0, "data_residency": 0, "cost_flexibility": 0}) == 0

q.en: What are you primarily choosing when you select Foundry vs Bedrock vs Vertex?

en: The operational home for AI workloads — governance, quotas, identity, observability, and billing

q.en: Which platform pairing correctly matches the 'wins by default' context?

en: Foundry for data-first shops; Vertex for Microsoft shops
Bedrock for M365 estates; Foundry for BigQuery estates
Foundry for Microsoft-centric, Bedrock for AWS-native, Vertex for data/ML-first organizations
All three are identical in every context

q.en: Why is choosing a platform from a model leaderboard a trap?

en: Model rankings churn within quarters while platform choice lasts years — keep models swappable behind a gateway instead
Platforms do not host leaderboard models

q.en: Why must criteria weights be agreed BEFORE scoring the platforms?

en: To prevent motivated arithmetic — tuning weights after seeing scores to produce a predetermined winner
Because scores must always be equal
